quiz_questions.question_text: What is Newton's first law of motion?

options.text: The net force on an object equals its mass multiplied by its acceleration, describing how force produces proportional changes in an object's velocity over time
For every action force there is an equal and opposite reaction force acting on a different object, as formalized in Newton's third law of interaction pairs
An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at constant velocity, unless acted upon by a net external force
Energy cannot be created or destroyed in an isolated system but can only be transformed from one form to another, conserving the total energy at all times

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's first law (law of inertia) states that an object's velocity remains constant (including zero) unless a net external force acts on it.

quiz_questions.question_text: Newton's first law is also known as the law of:

options.text: Acceleration
Action and reaction
Inertia
Conservation of energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's first law is called the law of inertia because it describes inertia — the tendency of objects to resist changes in their state of motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is inertia?

options.text: The tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion
The speed at which objects naturally move
The force that keeps objects moving
A type of friction

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertia is the natural resistance of an object to any change in its velocity. An object with more mass has more inertia.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which property of an object determines how much inertia it has?

options.text: Weight
Speed
Mass
Volume

quiz_questions.explanation: Mass is the quantitative measure of inertia. A more massive object has greater inertia and is harder to accelerate or decelerate.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hockey puck slides on frictionless ice. What happens to its velocity?

options.text: It speeds up over time due to the momentum stored inside it, because an object in motion naturally gains additional velocity even without an applied force
It remains constant — with no net force, the puck continues at the same velocity forever
It eventually comes to a complete stop after a certain time, because all moving objects naturally lose their velocity and return to a state of rest on any surface
It gradually slows down over time, because objects naturally lose velocity even in the absence of friction due to the inherent dissipation of motion energy

quiz_questions.explanation: On frictionless ice, there is no net force on the puck (gravity and normal force cancel vertically). Newton's first law dictates that the puck maintains its velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: If all forces on an object are balanced, what is its acceleration?

options.text: Equal to the applied force divided by mass
It depends on the object's speed
Zero
9.8 m/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: Balanced forces mean net force = 0. By Newton's second law (or first law), the acceleration is zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball rolls along a flat surface and eventually stops. Why does it stop?

options.text: It runs out of force
Gravity pulls it to a halt
Friction and air resistance (unbalanced forces) slow it down
Motion naturally stops on its own

quiz_questions.explanation: The ball decelerates because friction and air resistance provide a net backward force. Without these forces, it would continue rolling indefinitely (Newton's first law).

quiz_questions.question_text: A passenger in a car lurches forward when the car suddenly stops. This happens because of:

options.text: Gravity pulling the passenger forward
Inertia — the passenger's body tends to continue moving forward at the car's original velocity
A forward force from the engine
Air pressure inside the car

quiz_questions.explanation: The passenger's body was moving at the car's speed. When the car stops, the body continues forward due to inertia until a force (seatbelt, dashboard) stops it.

quiz_questions.question_text: Newton's first law applies in which type of reference frame?

options.text: Only rotating reference frames
Only the Earth's reference frame
All reference frames equally
Inertial reference frames — frames that are not accelerating

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's first law is valid only in inertial (non-accelerating) reference frames. In fact, an inertial frame is defined as one where Newton's first law holds.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object is moving at constant velocity. Must there be a net force on it?

options.text: Yes — friction must be overcome
Only if the object is heavy
No — constant velocity means zero acceleration, which means zero net force
Yes — a force is needed to maintain motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's first law: no net force is needed to maintain constant velocity. Only to change velocity (accelerate).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes Newton's first law?

options.text: The principle that F = ma
The principle that force is proportional to displacement
The principle that an object will not change its velocity unless a net external force causes it to accelerate
The principle that for every action there is a reaction

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's first law states that velocity is naturally constant — only a nonzero net force can change it. This applies to objects at rest (v = 0) and objects in motion (v ≠ 0).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes inertia?

options.text: The kinetic energy stored in a moving object due to its speed and mass, calculated as one-half times mass times velocity squared in the standard formula
The frictional resistance between two surfaces in contact, which depends on the coefficient of friction and the normal force pressing the surfaces together
The intrinsic property of matter that resists any change in velocity — an object's tendency to maintain its current state of rest or uniform motion
A force that actively opposes an object's motion, pushing back against the direction of travel and gradually reducing the object's speed until it comes to rest

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertia is not a force but a property of matter. Greater mass means greater inertia. A bowling ball has more inertia than a tennis ball — it is harder to start, stop, or redirect.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes mass in the context of Newton's first law?

options.text: A quantitative measure of an object's inertia — the resistance to changes in motion
The same as weight
The amount of space an object occupies
The gravitational pull on an object

quiz_questions.explanation: Mass measures inertia. Unlike weight (which depends on g), mass is the same everywhere. A 10 kg object has the same inertia on Earth, the Moon, or in deep space.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a balanced force system?

options.text: Forces that are all the same magnitude
A set of forces acting on an object whose vector sum is zero, resulting in no acceleration
Forces that all point in the same direction
A system with only one force

quiz_questions.explanation: When forces are balanced (ΣF = 0), there is no net force and no acceleration. The object is in equilibrium — either at rest or moving at constant velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an unbalanced force system?

options.text: Forces that are all oriented perpendicular to each other, forming right angles at the point of application and creating a complex multi-directional force pattern
A system containing exactly two forces of any magnitude and direction, since having precisely two forces is the defining characteristic of an unbalanced arrangement
A set of forces acting on an object whose vector sum is nonzero, resulting in acceleration in the direction of the net force
Forces in which one individual force is stronger in magnitude than the others, which always guarantees the presence of a nonzero net force on the object in question

quiz_questions.explanation: Unbalanced forces produce a nonzero net force, which causes acceleration (a = F_net/m). The acceleration is in the direction of the net force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the natural state of motion according to Newton?

options.text: Constant velocity (including rest) — an object free from net forces maintains its velocity indefinitely
Objects naturally accelerate on their own over time, building up speed spontaneously without requiring any external force to drive the increase in velocity
Objects naturally move in circular paths unless straightened out by an applied force, since circular motion is the default state of all matter in the universe
Everything in the universe naturally comes to rest eventually, because all motion gradually dies out on its own even when no retarding forces are present

quiz_questions.explanation: Before Newton, Aristotle believed rest was the natural state. Newton showed that constant velocity (including rest) is natural — only forces change velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an external force?

options.text: A force that only acts on the surface of an object
A force applied to a system from outside that system, capable of changing the system's momentum or energy
A force that originates inside the system
Any force that is very large

quiz_questions.explanation: External forces come from outside the defined system. Internal forces (within the system) cancel out. Only external forces can change the motion of the system's center of mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes static equilibrium?

options.text: A state where an object oscillates back and forth
A state where an object moves at constant velocity
A state in which an object is at rest and all forces acting on it are balanced (net force = 0, net torque = 0)
A state where all forces are zero individually

quiz_questions.explanation: Static equilibrium: the object is at rest (v = 0) and has zero acceleration (ΣF = 0). For extended objects, the net torque must also be zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes dynamic equilibrium?

options.text: A state where the forces acting on an object fluctuate rapidly in magnitude and direction, causing the object to vibrate or oscillate around a central position
A state where an object accelerates uniformly under a constant net force, gaining equal amounts of velocity during each equal time interval of the motion period
A state that can only be achieved in a perfect vacuum, since air molecules always prevent any real object from maintaining truly constant velocity in practice
A state in which an object moves at constant velocity with zero acceleration because all forces acting on it are balanced

quiz_questions.explanation: Dynamic equilibrium: the object moves at constant nonzero velocity (ΣF = 0, a = 0). A car cruising at constant speed on a straight road (engine force = friction + drag) is in dynamic equilibrium.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the law of inertia historically?

options.text: A law stating that heavier objects always move slower than lighter ones under the same conditions, because mass and speed are inversely proportional at all times
A law discovered by Albert Einstein as part of his theory of general relativity, describing how mass curves spacetime and affects the motion of nearby objects
The principle attributed to Galileo and formalized by Newton that objects naturally maintain their state of motion unless acted upon by an external force
A principle that only applies to objects drifting in outer space far from gravitational fields, and has no relevance to the motion of objects on planetary surfaces

quiz_questions.explanation: Galileo first recognized that objects don't naturally stop — friction is what stops them. Newton formalized this as his first law: without net force, velocity is constant.

quiz_questions.question_text: A magician pulls a tablecloth out from under dishes without disturbing them. Which law of physics explains this?

options.text: Newton's third law explains this trick — the cloth pushes the dishes upward with a reaction force that holds them in place while the cloth slides out horizontally
The law of conservation of energy explains this — the kinetic energy of the cloth is fully absorbed by the dishes, leaving them with no energy to move sideways
Newton's first law (inertia) — the dishes tend to remain at rest because no significant horizontal force acts on them during the quick pull
The law of gravity explains this — the gravitational force pressing the dishes against the table is much stronger than any horizontal pull from the sliding cloth

quiz_questions.explanation: The dishes have inertia — they resist changes to their state of rest. If the cloth is pulled quickly enough, friction between cloth and dishes acts for too brief a time to significantly accelerate the heavy dishes.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronaut in the International Space Station releases a wrench from her hand. What happens to it?

options.text: It accelerates away from the astronaut due to the residual air pressure inside the station, which pushes any unsupported objects toward the nearest wall or panel
It floats in place (or drifts at whatever velocity it had when released) — in the microgravity environment, there is no net force to change its motion
It immediately stops moving and remains frozen in the exact position where it was released, because objects in space have no momentum without active propulsion
It falls to the floor of the station just like on Earth, because the station's artificial gravity generator pulls all objects downward toward the lower deck surface

quiz_questions.explanation: In orbit, the station and everything inside are in free fall together. The wrench, once released, continues with whatever velocity it had — Newton's first law in action.

quiz_questions.question_text: A book sits on the dashboard of a car. When the car turns sharply left, the book slides to the right. Why?

options.text: The book's inertia keeps it moving in a straight line while the car turns beneath it — from the car's frame, it appears to slide right
Gravity shifts direction during the turn, pulling the book toward the right side of the car as the vehicle's orientation changes relative to the gravitational field
The dashboard has too much friction in that direction, which grabs the book's surface and drags it sideways toward the right as the car executes its left turn
A real rightward force pushes the book sideways across the dashboard, generated by the centripetal acceleration of the turning car acting directly on the object

quiz_questions.explanation: The car turns left, but the book, lacking sufficient friction to turn with it, continues in its original straight-line path (inertia). From inside the car, this looks like the book slides right.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two objects — a 1 kg ball and a 50 kg cart — are both at rest on a frictionless surface. You push each with 10 N for 2 seconds. Compare their responses.

options.text: The cart accelerates more because it is larger
Neither accelerates because the surface is frictionless
Both have the same acceleration because the same force is applied
Both accelerate, but the ball accelerates much more (10 m/s²) than the cart (0.2 m/s²) because the ball has less inertia

quiz_questions.explanation: Same force, different masses: a_ball = 10/1 = 10 m/s², a_cart = 10/50 = 0.2 m/s². The ball's smaller mass (less inertia) means it responds more to the same force.

quiz_questions.question_text: A satellite orbits Earth at constant speed. Is Newton's first law violated because the satellite changes direction?

options.text: No — the satellite's direction changes because gravity provides a continuous centripetal force. Newton's first law says constant velocity requires zero net force; here the net force (gravity) causes the change in direction
Yes the first law is violated — since the satellite moves at constant speed it should continue in a perfectly straight line, but instead it curves around Earth
Yes the first law is violated — constant speed always means zero net force, so the satellite should travel in a straight line rather than following a curved orbit
No the first law is not violated — but only because objects in outer space do not follow Newton's laws at all, since those laws apply exclusively to terrestrial motion

quiz_questions.explanation: The satellite moves at constant speed but not constant velocity — direction changes continuously. Gravity provides the centripetal force that changes the direction. Newton's first law is not violated because a net force (gravity) acts.

quiz_questions.question_text: You are ice skating and stop pushing. You glide for a long time before stopping. On a rough carpet, you would stop quickly. What does this demonstrate about Newton's first law?

options.text: It shows that ice surfaces have less gravitational pull than carpet surfaces, which is why objects appear to be lighter and move more freely when skating on ice
It demonstrates that objects naturally maintain their motion — the less friction (force), the longer the motion continues. Friction is what changes the state of motion, not a lack of force
It proves that Newton's first law only works on ice surfaces, since objects on other surfaces like carpet naturally come to rest without requiring any external force
It demonstrates that heavier people always stop faster on any surface, because mass is the primary factor determining how quickly a person decelerates to rest

quiz_questions.explanation: On ice (low friction), the net force opposing motion is small, so you decelerate slowly — closer to the ideal of Newton's first law. On carpet (high friction), the large opposing force decelerates you quickly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher places a penny on a card over a glass. She flicks the card away and the penny drops into the glass. Why doesn't the penny fly off with the card?

options.text: The penny has inertia — it resists the horizontal motion of the card. The quick flick doesn't transfer enough horizontal force to overcome the penny's inertia, so it stays in place and drops into the glass
The penny is simply too heavy for the card to move it, since the gravitational force pressing the penny downward exceeds any horizontal force the card could exert
The card actively pulls the penny downward into the glass as it slides away, using a lever-like mechanism to redirect the horizontal flicking force into a vertical drop
Air pressure above the penny holds it firmly in place while the card is removed, because atmospheric pressure is strong enough to prevent any horizontal displacement

quiz_questions.explanation: The card moves quickly, but the penny's inertia keeps it in its original position. With the card gone, gravity pulls the penny straight down into the glass. Classic Newton's first law demonstration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spacecraft engine fires, accelerating the ship. The engine then shuts off. Describe the subsequent motion, assuming deep space with no external forces.

options.text: The spacecraft immediately comes to a complete stop as soon as the engine shuts off, because without active propulsion there is nothing to sustain the forward motion
The spacecraft gradually slows down over time after the engine stops, because all moving objects naturally lose velocity and eventually return to a state of rest
The spacecraft continues to accelerate even after the engine shuts off, because the momentum stored during the engine burn continues to push it forward indefinitely
The spacecraft continues at the velocity it reached when the engine shut off — it moves in a straight line at constant speed forever (Newton's first law)

quiz_questions.explanation: In deep space, with no friction, gravity, or drag, the spacecraft experiences zero net force after the engine stops. By Newton's first law, it maintains constant velocity indefinitely.

quiz_questions.question_text: A container of water rests on a table in a moving train. Suddenly the train accelerates forward. What happens to the water surface?

options.text: The water evaporates due to the sudden acceleration of the train, because rapid velocity changes generate heat energy that raises the water temperature above boiling
The water surface remains perfectly level throughout the entire acceleration event, because water molecules are too heavy to be displaced by the train's forward motion
The water sloshes forward toward the front of the container, because the train's forward acceleration pulls the water along with it in the same direction of travel

The water surface tilts backward (higher at the back, lower at the front) because the water's inertia resists the forward acceleration — the water tends to stay behind while the container is pushed forward

quiz_questions.explanation: When the train accelerates, the container moves forward but the water resists due to inertia, causing it to pile up at the back. This is analogous to passengers being pushed back in their seats during acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two identical cars are parked side by side. Car A is empty (1000 kg) and Car B is fully loaded (2000 kg). Both are pushed with the same force. Why does Car A accelerate faster?

options.text: Car B has more friction between its tires and the road due to its greater weight, and this additional friction is the sole reason it is harder to push and accelerate
Car A has better quality tires that grip the road more effectively, allowing the pushing force to be transmitted to the ground more efficiently for greater acceleration
Car A has a stronger engine under its hood that supplements the pushing force, giving it an unfair advantage in acceleration despite receiving the same external push
Car A has less mass and therefore less inertia — with the same applied force, the less massive car experiences greater acceleration (a = F/m)

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's first law says objects resist changes in motion (inertia). Newton's second law quantifies it: a = F/m. For the same F, smaller m gives larger a. Car A, with half the mass, has half the inertia and twice the acceleration.
